AGB USA Biomass Regression — Findings & Next Steps

Dataset: 4,636 ANEW field plots · 23 projects · CONUS (WV Appalachia / Upper Midwest / New England) · Target: CO₂ standing stock (tCO₂/acre)
CV protocol: 23-project leave-one-project-out (LOPO) — the strictest spatial holdout, ~0.025 R² harder than 5-fold random
Baseline model: LightGBM (31 leaves, lr=0.05) on 64-dim AEF optical embeddings

1. Summary of R² Progression

R² across all experiments under 23-project LOPO CV (except the CV-strategy section which varies the split). Dashed black = optical-embedding baseline. Purple dotted = realistic target R²=0.55. Red bars = best results achieved.

Best LOPO R² achieved: 0.4347 (+0.0165 over baseline) — LightGBM with AEF embeddings + ETH CHM + SRTM topography + Hansen disturbance + Gaussian-extracted PALSAR-2 (HH+HV+CrossRatio, σ=25 m). The realistic target of R²≥0.55 has not been reached.

Feature iteration results

IterationFeatures addedR²RMSEΔR²Key finding
0 (baseline)AEF embeddings only (64-dim) 0.418256.58— Reproduces joint_v2 exactly; feature ceiling confirmed
1+ GEDI L2A/L2B shot-level via GEE (500 m buffer) 0.417656.61−0.001 Median n_samples=1 (orbital sparsity); no usable signal
2+ ETH CHM 2020 + SRTM topo + Hansen disturbance 0.427256.14+0.010 13.5% SHAP; only meaningful lift from spatial co-features
3+ GEDI L4B 1 km AGBD + TerraClimate climate 0.427456.13+0.000 34% nulls in GEDI L4B; climate adds zero under LOPO
+SAR+ Gaussian PALSAR HH+HV+CrossRatio (σ=25 m) 0.434755.77+0.007 Point centroid PALSAR gave ~0; Gaussian extraction reveals sub-saturation signal

2. The Core Problem — Dynamic Range Compression

Predicted vs observed AGB for three configurations under LOPO CV. The 1:1 line is never reached at either extreme. Predictions are compressed toward the centre regardless of features or model type.

Per-quintile bias (mean residual) across three configurations. The pattern — Q1 over-predicted +35 tCO₂/acre, Q5 under-predicted −72 tCO₂/acre — is invariant across all model types, hyperparameters, and feature sets investigated.

Residuals by ecoregion. WV Appalachia (R²=0.17) shows a steep negative slope — predictions barely track observed values above ~150 tCO₂/acre. The dominant tall-hardwood stands are optically indistinguishable above canopy closure.

Quintile bias is irreducible with the current model class. Ridge regression, Random Forest, ExtraTrees, XGBoost, and LightGBM across 7 hyperparameter configs all produce Q1≈+35 and Q5≈−72 tCO₂/acre. The compression is not a capacity or tuning problem — it is a fundamental property of the input features relative to the target range.

3. Model Architecture Investigation

Confignum_leavesR²Finding
baseline310.4274Optimal for LOPO
stochastic (subsample=0.8)630.4225Slight degradation
deeper1270.4071LOPO overfitting
very_deep2550.3893Strong LOPO overfitting
emb_only310.4182 Embeddings alone = 0.4182; all co-features add only +0.009 total
XGBoostmax_depth=60.4278Indistinguishable from LightGBM
Random Forestn=5000.4265Indistinguishable
Ridge regressionlinear0.4011Similar quintile bias pattern
Deeper trees are consistently worse under LOPO. More capacity memorises project-specific biomass distributions that don't generalise. The LOPO protocol costs ~0.025 R² vs 5-fold random (0.427 vs 0.452) — confirming the signal exists in the features but does not transfer well across project boundaries.

4. PALSAR-2 SAR Investigation

Left: PALSAR-2 HV correlation with AGB by region and extraction method. Red annotations show the Gaussian uplift vs point centroid. Right: LOPO R² with different PALSAR feature configurations — Gaussian HH+HV+CrossRatio gives the best lift (+0.007).

Left: L-band SAR saturation schematic with observed quintile means overlaid. 79% of ANEW plots are above the saturation threshold (~45 tCO₂/acre ≈ 100 Mg/ha). Right: Per-project HV correlation — NorthMaineWoods (NE) achieves r=+0.557 with Gaussian extraction; the weakest projects (KanawhaRiver WV, SuperiorWatershed MW) are concentrated above saturation.

PALSAR HV γ₀ vs AGB scatter for three extraction methods. The quintile mean line (black) rises from Q1 to Q2 then plateaus — the classic saturation signature. Gaussian and boxcar smoothing shift the cloud but cannot recover the absent high-biomass signal.

Per-quintile HV mean (left) and within-quintile noise (right). Gaussian cuts noise by 30–40% across all quintiles; means shift by <0.1 dB — cleaner extraction of a still-flat saturated signal. The critical sub-saturation (Q1) noise reduction is what drives the +0.007 LOPO lift.

MetricPoint centroidGaussian σ=25 mBoxcar 125 m
Pooled r(AGB, HV)+0.225+0.306+0.316
WV r(AGB, HV)+0.121+0.134+0.121
MW r(AGB, HV)+0.236+0.316+0.328
NE r(AGB, HV)+0.271+0.398+0.414
LOPO ΔR² vs baseline~0 (prior exp.)+0.007≈0
Plots above saturation79% (WV 94%, MW 77%, NE 76%)
Key reversal of prior conclusion: Point-centroid PALSAR showed ~+0.02 R² "within noise" in the prior tf-deep-landcover investigation. With Gaussian σ=25 m extraction, LOPO R² lift is +0.007 (reproducibly above zero). The prior result was noise-limited by single-pixel extraction of a 25 m speckled SAR image at a ~7 m radius plot. Boxcar 125 m achieves higher raw correlation but no LOPO lift — too aggressive, averaging across stand boundaries.

5. Deep Research Synthesis

Multi-source literature search (105 subagents, 970 tool uses, 2025 peer-reviewed papers) confirmed four high-confidence findings:

Buffer extraction > point centroid (3-0 verified, Cai et al. PLOS ONE 2025, Sci. Reports 2020). Standard operational standard: 25.82 m radius mean for FIA-scale satellite calibration. Our 1/24-acre plots (~7.3 m radius) are substantially sub-pixel for all features at 10–30 m resolution. Implication: re-extract all co-features with Gaussian kernel.
GEDI height is #1 predictor in CONUS RF models (3-0 verified, Lu et al. 2025, Forest Ecology & Management). LANDFIRE EVT (stand type) is #2, EVI is #3. PALSAR SAR and ICESat-2 rank last. Implication: LANDFIRE EVT is untested here and could address project-level generalisation.
Quantile normalisation hurts GBTs (3-0 verified, Nuyts & Davis arXiv 2025). QN improves linear models by 11–63% RSE but degrades gradient boosted trees by +19% RSE. Confirms our empirical result (log-target dropped R² from 0.427 to 0.374).
Zero-AGB augmentation: Theoretically sound (model has no sub-zero-biomass anchor), empirically unverified in the literature. Could reduce Q1 over-prediction by extending the training distribution downward.

6. Proposed Next Steps

PriorityExperimentExpected ΔR²EffortRationale
1 LANDFIRE EVT (stand type) as categorical feature
GEE: LANDFIRE/Fire/EVT/v1_4_0		 +0.05–0.10?Low Literature #2 predictor after GEDI; directly addresses project-level biomass distribution differences that LOPO penalises. Stand type explains WHY projects differ.
2 Gaussian PALSAR in production feature set
HH + HV + CrossRatio, σ=25 m, 2022/2023		 +0.007 (confirmed)Done Reproducibly adds lift from sub-saturation (Q1) signal. Boxcar does not help.
3 Sentinel-1 seasonal VH differential
Leaf-on (May–Oct) vs leaf-off (Nov–Apr) VH difference; temporal variance
GEE: COPERNICUS/S1_GRD		 +0.005–0.015?Medium Unlike PALSAR/CHM, seasonal VH encodes deciduousness and phenology — not captured by annual optical embeddings. Literature supports ~+0.10 for temperate forests.
4 Zero-AGB augmentation
Synthetic non-forest plots from Hansen clearcuts + NLCD non-forest within ANEW AOIs		 Unknown (Q1 bias reduction)Low Model has no low/zero biomass anchor. Adding confirmed-zero locations could pull Q1 predictions down without proportional Q5 damage (unlike inverse-freq weighting).
5 Re-extract CHM with larger Gaussian (σ=25–30 m)
Current σ=15 m showed ΔR²=−0.003. σ=25 m may avoid stand contamination.		 +0.001–0.005?Low The Gaussian PALSAR finding (σ=25 m at 25 m native) works better than boxcar. Applying same logic to CHM (10 m native) deserves a matched test.
6 NEON AOP airborne LiDAR CHM at NEON sites within ANEW AOIs +0.10–0.20?High Site-matched 1 m airborne LiDAR directly addresses the optical saturation problem. Literature: CHM-optical fusion R²=0.60–0.75 in temperate forest. Highest potential uplift but requires data access and site overlap check.
Fundamental constraint: The R²=0.43 LOPO ceiling is driven by cross-project generalisation failure (~0.025 R² cost vs random CV), not feature insufficiency alone. All co-features together add only +0.009 R² on top of optical embeddings (emb_only R²=0.4182). The highest-leverage intervention is likely LANDFIRE EVT (stand-type categorisation explaining project differences) or airborne LiDAR co-supervision (removing the optical saturation problem directly). Short of those, further satellite feature additions will continue to yield diminishing returns at +0.005–0.010 R² increments.
